docs.title: Operations, Relations and Notations
docs.text:
[!info] Union

The union \(E ∪ F\) of two sets \(E, F\) is the set of points that belong to at least one of them.

Symbolically,\(E\cup F\)={ \(\omega | \omega\in E \ or \ \omega \in F\) }

\(E\cup F\) is realized if either \(E\) or \(F\) occurs.

[!info] Intersection

The intersection \(E ∩ F( or EF)\) of two sets \(E, F\) is the set of points that belong to both of them.

Symbolically,\(EF\)={\(\omega | \omega\in E \ and \ \omega \in F\) }

\(EF\) is realized if \(E\) and \(F\) both occur.

[!info] Countable union/intersection \(\{E_i\}_{i\ge 1}\) is a countable sequence of events.

  • The union of these events denoted \(\bigcup\limits_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i\) is defined to be the event which consists of all outcomes that are in at least one event \(E_i\) ,\(i=1,2...,\infty\)

\(\omega \in \bigcup\limits_{1}^{\infty}E_i\Longleftrightarrow \exists i \ s.t. \omega \in E_i\)

  • The intersection of these events denoted \(\bigcap\limits_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i\) is defined to be the event which consists of all outcomes that are in all of the events \(E_i\) ,\(i=1,2...,\infty\)

\(\omega \in \bigcap\limits_{1}^{\infty}E_i\Longleftrightarrow \omega \in E_i \ \forall \ i\)

[!info] Mutually exclusive \(E,F\) are mutually exclusive if \(E\cap F= \emptyset\)

\(E\) and \(F\) can not both occur at the same time.

[!info] Complement For a set \(E \subset S\) ,the complement of \(E\) is denoted by \(E^c\) and is the set of points that do not belong to \(E\).

Symbolically,\(E^c=\{ \omega \in S|\omega \notin E\}\) \(E\cup E^c=S,E \cap E^c=\emptyset,(E^c)^c=E\)

[!info] Difference For events E, F, \(E\setminus F\) is the set of points that belong to E but not to F.

Symbolically,\(E\setminus F=\{\omega| \omega \in E\ and \ \omega \notin F\}\)

\(E\setminus F =E\setminus(E\cap F),E^c=S\setminus E\)

[!info] Symmetric difference

\(E \bigtriangleup F\) is the set of points that belong to exactly one of them.

Symbolically,\(E \bigtriangleup F =\{ \omega| \omega \in E\setminus F \ or \ \omega \in F\setminus E\}\)

[!important] [[Venn diagram]] \(\mathcal{S}\):a large rectangle events \(E,F\)... :represented by circles events of interests: shading the appropriate regions of the diagram`

docs.title: Laws on sets
docs.text:
[!info] Commutativity \(E\cup F=F \cup E \ and \ E \cap F=F \cap E\)

[!info] Associativity \(E \cup(F\cup G)=(E\cup F)\cup G \ and \ E\cap(F \cap G)=(E\cap F) \cap G\)

[!info] Distributivity

\(E \cup(F\cap G)=(E\cup F)\cap (E\cup G) \ and \ E\cap(F \cup G)=(E\cap F) \cup (E \cap G)\)

[!info] Transitivity

If \(E \subset F \ and\ F \subset G\) ,then \(E \subset G\)

[!NOTE] Proposition 2.5 ([[De Morgan’s laws]]) \((1)( igcup\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i)^c= igcap\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i^c\)

\((2)( igcap\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i)^c= igcup\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i^c\)

Proof

It suffices to show (1) since if (1) holds , then we consider \(( igcup\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i^c)^c= igcap\limits_{i=1}^{n}(E_i^c)^c= igcap\limits_{i=1}^{n}E_i\)

We get (2) by applying complement to both sides.

So we only need to proof (1)

\(\subset\)

\(\omega otin igcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i\) , For any \(\omega otin E_i\) >> \(\omega \in E_i^c \ for \ all \ i\)

\(s.t. \ \omega \in igcap_{i=1}^{n}E_i^c\)

==**\(\supset\)

docs.title: [[σ-algebra]] and [[Measurable space]]
docs.text:
[!NOTE] Definition 2.6 ([[σ-algebra]]) A σ-algebra A ⊂ P(S) is a family of sets over S satisfying the following properties: + \(\emptyset \in \mathcal{A}\) + \(\mathcal{A}\) is closed under complementation: If \(E \in \mathcal{A}\) , then \(E^c \in \mathcal{A}\) , \(\mathcal{A}\) is closed under countable union:if \((E_i)_{i\ge1}\)is a countable sequence of sets in \(\mathcal{A}\) ( \(E_i\in \mathcal{A}\ for \ i\in N^*)\), then \(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i\in \mathcal{A}\)

[!NOTE] Definition 2.7 ([[Measurable space]]) Let us consider a random experiment with sample space \(S\) endowed with \(\sigma - algebra \ \mathcal{A}\), \((S,\mathcal{A})\) is called a measurable space and elements of \(\mathcal{A}\) are called events.

Under this is the most important part——Axioms of Probability

docs.title: Axioms
docs.text:
[!NOTE] Definition 2.8 (Probability, [[Kolmogorov]], 1933) Let \((S, \mathcal{A})\) be a measurable space of events. A [[probability measure]] is a real-valued function mapping \(\mathcal{P} : \mathcal{A} → \mathcal{R}\) satisfying: + for any event \(E\in \mathcal{A},\mathcal{P}\in(E)\ge0\) (nonnegativity) + \(\mathcal{P}(S)=1\)

  • for any countably infinite sequence of events \((E_i)_{i\ge1}\) that are mutually exclusive, $$ \mathcal{P}(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\mathcal{P}(E_i) $$ (\(\sigma\)-additivity or countable addtivity) Then \((S,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{P})\) is called a [[probability space]].

docs.title: Some properties
docs.text:
[!NOTE] Property 1 (Probability of the impossible event) $$ P(\emptyset)=0 $$

Proof

Let\(\ E_1=S\) ,\(E_i=\emptyset \ for\ all \ i\ge2\), \(\{ E_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty} \ is \ a \ mutually \ exclusive \ sequence\) $$ \ \begin{aligned} P\left( \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i \right)&=P(S)\ &\overset{(3)}{=}\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(E_i)=P(S)+\sum_{i=2}^{\infty}P(\emptyset) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \sum_{i=2}^{\infty}P(\emptyset)=0 \overset{(1)}{\to} P(\emptyset)=0 $$

[!NOTE] Property 2 (Probability of a finite union of mutually exclusive events) For any finite sequence of events \(E_1,...,E_n\in \mathcal{A}\) that are mutually exclusive (that is, \(E_i\cap E_j=\emptyset \ if \ i \neq j\)), $$ P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}P(E_i) $$

Proof

Let \(E_k=\emptyset\) for all \(k>n\) \(\{E_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}\) countable, mutually exclusive $$ \begin{align} P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i) &\overset{(3)}{=}\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(E_i) \&=\sum_{i=1}^{n}P(E_i)+\sum_{i=n+1}^{\infty}P(\emptyset) \&=\sum_{i=1}^{n}P(E_i) \end{align} $$

[!NOTE] Property 3 (Probability of included events)

For any two events \(E, F \in A\), $$ E \subset F \Rightarrow P(E)\le P(F). $$

Proof $$ E\subset F F=E\cup (F\setminus E) \P(F)=P(E)+{\color{Blue}P(F\setminus E)\overset{(1)}{\ge}0} \P(F)\ge P(E) $$

[!NOTE] Property 4

For any event \(E\in \mathcal{A}\), $$ P(E)\le 1 $$

Proof $$ E\subset S \overset{Property 3}{\Rightarrow}P(E)\le 1 $$

[!NOTE] Property 5 (Law of total probability) Let \(F\in \mathcal{A}\) be an event and \((E_i)_{i\ge 1}\) be a countable partition of the sample space \(S\) (that is, \(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i=S\ and \ E_i\cap E_j=\emptyset \ for \ i \neq j\)), $$ P(F)=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(F\cap E_i) $$

Proof $$ \begin{align} P(F)&=P(F\cap S) \&=P(F\cap (\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)) \&=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}F\cap E_i) \&\overset{(3)}{=}\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(F\cap E_i) \end{align} $$ $$ because { F\cap E_i}_{i=1}^{\infty} is mutually exclusive sequence $$

[!NOTE] Property 6 (Probability of the complement)

For any event \(E\in \mathcal{A}\), $$ P(E^c)=1-P(E) $$

Proof $$ 1\overset{(2)}{=}P(S)=P(E\cup E^c)\overset{property 2}{=}P(E)+P(E^c) $$

[!NOTE] Property 7 (Probability of the union of 2 events)

For any two events \(E,F\in \mathcal{A}\), $$ P(E\cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E\cap F) $$

Proof $$ \begin{align} F&=(F\cap E)\cup(F\cap E^c)

\(F\cap E)\cup(F\cap E^c) &is mutually exclusive \

P(F)&=P(F\cap E)+{\color{Red}P(F\cap E^c)} \from E\cup F&=E\cup (F\cap E^c) \P(E\cup F)&=P(E)+{\color{Red}P(F\cap E^c)} \ &=P(E)+P(F)-P(E\cap F) \end{align}

\ $$

[!NOTE] Property 8 ([[Inclusion-Exclusion Identity]]/[[Poincaré’s formula]])

For any two events \(E,F\in \mathcal{A}\), $$ P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}(-1)^{k-1}\sum_{1\le i_1 < ...< i_k\le n}P(E_{i_1}\cap...\cap E_{i_k}), $$ where \(\sum\limits_{1\le i_1<...<i_k\le n}\) means the sum for all subsets of \((1,...,n)\) of size k.

Proof

Poincare's formula proved by induction

[!NOTE] Property 9

For events \(E,F\) $$ P(E\cup F)\le P(E)+P(F) $$

Proof $$ \begin{align} P(E\cup F)&=P(E)+P(F)-P(E\cap F) \&\le P(E)+P(F) \end{align} $$

[!NOTE] Property 10 (A generalization)

For a finite sequence of events \(E_1,...,E_n,\) $$ P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i)\le \sum_{i=1}^{n}P(E_i)=P(E_i)+...+P(E_n) $$

Proof $$ \begin{align} P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)&=P(E_1\cup(\bigcup_{i=2}^{\infty}E_i)) \&\le P(E_1)+P(\bigcup_{i=2}^{\infty}E_i) \end{align} $$

[!NOTE] Property 11 ([[Boole’s Inequality]]) For a countable infinite sequence of events \(\{E_i\}_{i\ge1}\) $$ P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)\le\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(E_i) $$

Proof

We construct a new sequence of events \(\{F_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty}\) mutually exclusive. $$ \begin{align}

Make F_1=E_1 F_2=E_2 \setminus E_1 & F_k=E_k \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{k-1}E_i (k\ge2) \\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i=\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}F_i,& \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i=\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}F_i \Obviously,the { F_i}_{i=1}^{\infty} &are mutually exclusive\

P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)&=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}F_i) \&=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(F_i) \&\overset{F_k \subset E_k}{\le}\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(E_i) \end{align} $$

[!example] $$ \color{Blue}Motivation example:Infinitely large urn and infinite balls $$

Suppose we have an infinitely large urn, and an infinite collection of balls labeled as number 1, 2, 3, and so on. Consider an experiment as follows:

  • At 1 minute to 12pm, balls numbered 1 through 10 are placed in the urn and ball number 1 is withdrawn;
  • At \(\frac{1}{2}\) to 12pm, balls numbered 11 through 20 are placed in the urn and ball number 2 withdrawn;
  • At \(\frac{1}{4}\) to 12pm, balls numbered 21 through 30 are placed in the urn and ball number 3 withdrawn;
  • ...

and so on.How many balls are there in the urn at 12pm ?

(the answer is 0 and why?)

If we place the 1 to 10 balls and withdraw 10,place the 11 to 20 balls and withdraw 20,there are infinite balls(contrast two situations).

docs.title: Continuity property
docs.text:
[!NOTE] Definition 2.9 (Increasing/Decreasing sequences)

A sequence of events \(\{E_n,n\ge1\}\) is said to be an increasing sequence if $$ E_1\subset E_2...\subset E_n\subset ..., $$ and we define a new event { \(\lim\limits_{n\to \infty}E_n \ by \ \lim\limits_{n\to \infty}E_n=\bigcup\limits_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n\) }

A sequence of events \(\{E_n,n\ge1\}\) is said to be an increasing sequence if $$ E_1\supset E_2...\supset E_n\supset ..., $$ and we define a new event \({\lim\limits_{n\to \infty}}E_n \ by \ \lim\limits_{n\to \infty}E_n=\bigcap\limits_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n\)

[!NOTE] Proposition 2.10 (Continuity property)

If \(\{E_n,n\ge1\}\) is either an increasing or a decreasing sequence, then $$ \lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n)=P(\lim_{n\to\infty }E_n) $$

^pro2-10

Proof

It suffices to discuss the increasing case since if ,

\(\{E_n \ n\ge1\}\)is decreasing the \(\{E_n^c \ n\ge1 \}\) is increasing. $$ \begin{align} \lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n^c)\overset{increasing}{=}P(\lim_{n\to\infty}E_n^c)=P(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n^c)=P((\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n)^c)=1-P(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n) \\lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n^c)=1-\lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n)=1-P(\lim_{n\to\infty}E_n)=1-P(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n) \\lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n)=P(\lim_{n\to\infty}E_n) \ \end{align} $$ Now we prove the result for increasing \(E_n\) ,we do the same construction as that in Proposition 10[[#^pro2-10]].

$$ \begin{align} F_n&=E_n\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1}E_i=E_n\setminus E_{n-1} \

RHS&=P(\lim_{n\to\infty}E_n)=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty}E_i)=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^\infty F_i) \&=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}P(F_i)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{i=1}^{n}P(F_i) \&=\lim_{n\to\infty}P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}F_i)=\lim_{n\to\infty}P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}E_i)=\lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n)

\end{align} $$

Now let's focus on original example. We change the ball withdrawn to randomly.

We consider the ball number 1. Let \(E_n\) be the event that ball 1 is still in the urn after the \(n^{th}\) withdraw. $$ \begin{align} \ E_1\subset E_2\subset E_3 &is decreasing\

P(ball 1 is in the urn at 12pm)&=P(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}E_n) \&=\lim_{n\to\infty}P(E_n) \&=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{9}{10}·\frac{18}{19}···=\lim_{n\to\infty}\prod_{k=1}^{n}\frac{9k}{9k+1} \&=\lim_{n\to\infty}exp(\sum_{k=1}^{n}ln\frac{9k}{9k+1}) \&=0 (ln\frac{9k}{9k+1}\sim-\frac{1}{9k+1}) \end{align} $$ The discussion for other balls is the same . So we can get the conclusion. $$ \begin{align} & P(the urn is not empty at 12pm) \&=P(there is at least one ball in the urn at 12pm) \&=P(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}F_n) (where F_n ={ball number n is in the urn at 12pm)} \&\overset{Boole's Inequality}{\le}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}P(F_n) \&\overset{Axiom 1}{=}0 \end{align} $$

docs.title: Uniform probability measure
docs.text:
We first study finite sample space

[!NOTE] Definition 2.9 (uniform probability measure)

Let \(S\) be a finite sample space \(S = \{\omega_1,...,\omega_n\}\) with \(|S|=n\in N,n\ge1\) and \((S,P(S),P)\) be a probability space. Probability measure \(P\) is said to be uniform if all outcomes \(\omega_i\) in the sample space are equally likely to occur,that is,\(P(\{\omega_i\})=\alpha\) for \(i=1,...,n,\) with \(\alpha\ge 0\) .

[!NOTE] Properties

Let \((S,P(S),P)\) be a probability space with uniform probability measure \(P\) on a finite sample space \(S=\{ \omega_1,...,\omega _n\}\). Then $$ \begin{align} &(1)P({\omega_i})=\frac{1}{n},for i =1,...,n. \&(2)For any event E,P(E)=\frac{|E|}{|S|} \end{align} $$

Proof $$ \begin{eqnarray} \&(1)S={\omega_1}\cup{\omega_2}···\cup{\omega_n} \ &1=P(S)=P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n}\omega_i)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}P(\omega_i)=n\alpha& \&\alpha=\frac{1}{n} \ &(2)P(E)=P(\bigcup_{i\in(1,...,n) \omega_i\in E}{\omega_i})=\sum_{i\in(1,2...n) \omega_i\in E} P({\omega_i}) \&=\frac{|E|\frac{1}{n}}{n·\frac{1}{n}}=\frac{|E|}{n} \end{eqnarray} $$

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
[!question] Poker problem

In the game of bridge, the entire deck of 52 cards is dealt out to 4 players. What is the probability that

  • one of the players receives all 13 spades;
  • each player receives 1 ace?

Solution

\(S=\{all \ possibel\ distribution\ to \ 4\ players\}\) \(|S|=\binom{52}{13,13,13,13}\) $$ \begin{align} 1.E&={one of the players get all 13 spades} \&=\bigcup_{i=1}^{4}{player i gets all 13 spades} \&=\sum_{i=1}^{4}P(player i gets 13 spades) \&=4\frac{\binom{39}{13,13,13}}{\binom{52}{13,13,13,13}} \2.F&={each player gets one ace} \&P(F)=\frac{|F|}{|S|}=\frac{4\binom{48}{12,12,12,12}}{\binom{52}{13,13,13,13}} \ \end{align} $$

[!question] [[Birthday Problem]] Recall that Pigeonhole principle tells us that among 366 people, there are at least 2 people celebrating their birthday on the same day (we ignore the Feb. 29 case). Now we want to study for n people, what is the probability that no two of them celebrate their birthday on the same day? How large need n be so that this probability is less than \(\frac{1}{2}\) ?

Solution

For n people,\(S=\{all \ possibel\ birthday\ of \ n\ people\}\)

\(|S|=365^n\) $$ \begin{align} & P(no two of them have the same birthday) \&=\frac{365·364···(365-n+)}{365^n}<\frac{1}{2} when n\ge23 \&when n=100,the probability>\frac{3\times10^6}{3\times10^6+1} \end{align} $$

[!question] [[Matching Problem]] Suppose that each of N men at a party throw his hat into the center of the room. The hats are first mixed up, and then each man randomly selects a hat. What is the probability that none of the men selects his own hat?

^matching-problem

Solution

We analysis the complement event at least one man selects his own hats

Let \(E_i=the \ i^{th}\ man\ selects\ his\ own\ hats\) $$ \begin{align} P(\bigcup_{i}^{N}E_i)&=\sum_{i=1}^{N}P(E_i)-\sum_{1\le i_1\le i_2...\le N}P(E_{i_1}E_{i_2}...)+...+(-1)^{N+1}P(E_1..E_N) \end{align} $$ Note that \(E_{i_1}E_{i_2}..E_{i_n}\) represents the event that \(i_1^{th}...i_{n}^{th}\) men get their own hats $$ \begin{align} P(E_{i_1}E_{i_2}..E_{i_n})&=\frac{(N-n)!}{N!} \\sum_{1\le i_1<i_2...<i_N\le N }P(E_{i_1}E_{i_2}..E_{i_n})&=\binom{N}{n}\frac{(N-n)!}{N!}\frac{1}{N!}=\frac{1}{n!} \P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{N}E_i)&=1-\frac{1}{2!}+\frac{1}{3!}+...+(-1)^{N+1}\frac{1}{N!} \&=\sum_{k=1}^{N}(-1)^{k+1}\frac{1}{k!} \end{align} $$ the probability that no one selects his own hat is $$ \begin{align} 1-P(\bigcup_{i=1}^{N}E_i)&=1-\sum_{k=1}^{N}(-1)^{k+1}\frac{1}{k!}\ &=\sum_{k=0}^{N}(-1)^k\frac{1}{k!}\overset{N\to\infty}{\to}e^{-1} \end{align} $$ Recall the [[Talor expansion]] of \(e^x=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^k}{k!}\) When N is large the probability is close to \(e^{-1}\approx0.3679\)

docs.title: HW5
docs.text:
1. 两支队伍进行比赛，首先取得四场胜利的队伍是胜者，其中强队获胜概率为0.6，每场胜利独立，计算\(i=4,5,6,7\) 时，强队正好在第\(i\)场获胜的概率。比较七局四胜和三局两胜制的胜率大小。 $$ \mathbb{P}_{i}=\binom{i-1}{3}(0.6)^{4}(0.4)^{i-4} $$ 其中二项系数为\(i-1\)而不是\(i\)原因在于最后一场的胜负已经被确定。

Not Wrong, but use the method; 2. 一个盒子内有10个红球，8个黑球，7个绿球。任选其中一种颜色，抓到后记1，反之0，在其中随机抓取4个球,以\(X\)指代抓到选中颜色的球的个数计算： (a)\(\mathbb{P}(X=0)\) (b)计算\(\mathbb{P}(X_{i}=1),i=1,2,3,4\) (c)计算\(\mathbb{E}[X]\)

补充内容： Self-Test 4.20 ![[A first course in probability Eighth Edition = 概率论基础教程 (英文版·第8版) (Sheldon M. Ross) (Z-Library).pdf#page=181&rect=259,238,497,340|A first course in probability Eighth Edition = 概率论基础教程 (英文版·第8版) (Sheldon M. Ross) (Z-Library), p.174]] $$ \begin{align} \mathbb{E}\left[ \frac{1}{X} \right] & =\sum\limits_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(1-p)^{k-1}}{k}p \ & =\frac{p}{1-p}\sum\limits_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(1-p)^{k}}{k} \ & =\frac{p}{1-p}\sum\limits_{k=1}^{\infty} \int {0}^{1-p}x^{k-1} \, dx \ & = \frac{p}{1-p}\int \, dx \}^{1-p}\sum\limits_{k=1}^{\infty} x^{k-1 & =\frac{p}{1-p}\int_{0}^{1-p} \frac{1}{1-x}dx \ & =\frac{-p\ln p}{1-p} \end{align} $$ 利用级数的一致收敛性，交换积分和求和的顺序是一种有用的技巧

Self-Test 4.25 For the match problem,find (a) the expected number of matches. (b) the variance of the number of matches.

(a): Use the method in the HW5_7 也就是将抓到与否改为0-1的分布 那么我们可以将模型修改为（1，\(\frac{1}{n}\)）,那么很自然地可以有\(\mathbb{E}[X]=\mathbb{E}\left[ \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}x_{i} \right]=1\) 这个问题的关键在于运用HW5_7的方法改为0-1分布，每个人抓到自己帽子的概率都是一样大的（在标号的前提下）

关于帽子问题，由于假定条件应该是在\(n!\)种情况中发生概率均等（古典的看法），所以可以采用计算样本点个数的方法。（测度？？？）还用到了一个概率与顺序无关的理念，粗略说来是由于对称性，每个人得到自己的帽子概率应该是相同的，因为处于每个位置的可以是任何人！

(b): 在独立情况下，方差可以直接相加，不独立情况下要考虑协方差（Not learned）。

Remark 一个修正：在用连续型随机变量估计离散型随机变量时，例如运用正态分布进行估计时，我们要将离散的点纳入考量，例如\(X>150\) 中，我们运用连续型随机变量没有区别但是实际上相差了一个\(\mathbb{P}(X=150)\)的值，我们就应该考虑向左移0.5个单位。

Theoretical 5.2 一些特殊的期望表达式： 离散版本：\(\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(N>n)\) 连续版本：\(\mathbb{E}[N]=\int _{0}^\infty \, \mathbb{P}(X>x)dx\)-\(\int_{0}^\infty \mathbb{P}(X<-x)dx\) 离散版本的证明是Ross书中的一道习题，连续版本同样 我们下面对连续版本进行证明 $$ \begin{align} \int_{0}^\infty \mathbb{P}(Y>y)dy & =\int_{0}^\infty \int_{y}^\infty P(Y=x)dxdy \ & =\int_{0}^\infty \int_{0}^x P(Y=x)dydx \ & =\int_{0}^\infty xP(Y=x)dx \end{align} $$ 综合两边可得 $$ \int_{0}^\infty \mathbb{P}(X>x)dx-\int_{0}^\infty \mathbb{P}(X<-x)dx=\int_{0}^\infty xP(Y=x)dx+\int_{-\infty}^0xP(Y=x)=\mathbb{E}[N] $$

Self-Test 4.22 比赛五场，胜利概率p，第五场赢了将一直玩到输，计算 + 玩的局数的期望 + 输了的局数的期望 转化问题为一个几何分布,第一问抛开前四局，第二问则是抛开第五局后的部分： 设玩的局数为Y，那么\((Y-4)\)~\(Geom（1-p\)）,期望按照几何分布计算即可 输了的期望就是4(1-p)+1
